focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The Haviland Innovation Atrium smelled of lemon polish, espresso, and pastry that had been thawed somewhere else. By nine in the morning sun through the glass walls laid a hard white shine across the concrete floor and made the donor banners look more expensive than cloth had any right to look. The room had been built to flatter engineering. Every cord was hidden. Every table edge matched. Even the fingerprints disappeared.

Leela stood behind a retractable wall near the catering entrance with a paper folder tucked under her arm and her laptop open on a high-top table that was supposed to hold sparkling water. Her right wrist ached inside the brace. The folder held twelve printed packets, the public version of what Marisol had checksummed into a drawer in March, because Omar had insisted that if the wireless failed or somebody decided to be brave in theory and useless in practice, paper still counted as technology. Her phone showed three threads open at once: Omar, June, and Nina Solis from the graduate workers union, who had the encrypted archive and instructions to send it wider if all of them lost their nerve at the same time.

June arrived first, coming through the service door with a paper cup of coffee she did not seem to want. Her blunt-cut hair had been hacked shorter again, the line at one side a little crooked. She stopped long enough to flatten the cup between her hands and drop it into the trash before she said, "How many backup plans are we on now."

"Three send paths, two physical packets, and Nina if both of those fail," Leela said. "Four, if you count the timed link."

"That one doesn't count. Timed links are for people who still believe in elegant solutions."

She had nicotine gum in one pocket and her yellow legal pad under the other arm....
prev_tail_excerpt:
...iting the inside of her cheek without noticing. Now she found the torn place with her teeth and bit down deliberately, hard enough to make the pain separate from everything else. It was an ugly, bodily impulse, the kind a body chose when it needed one sharp signal. Skin gave. Warm salt flooded her mouth over the chalk of the antacid she had taken that morning.

She spat into the sink. Pink first, then darker.

The automatic faucet took a second to recognize her hands. Water jumped out too fast, cold enough to sting. She rinsed and spat again. A thin clot clung to the white basin before the stream worried it toward the drain.

Her phone vibrated in the bag. Once. Then again.

She did not look.

She took Mohit's packet out and laid it on the counter because the bag suddenly felt too full of paper, too much like an office she was carrying on her shoulder. The top page was already bent where she had jammed it in. Pending revision and further review, the recommendation line said. A drop of blood fell from her mouth before she could turn away and stippled the words further review. Toner feathered outward in a tiny gray bruise.

The words blurred under the blood, and with them went the last hope that the right phrasing might still save her.

She bit the cheek again. Harder this time. The fresh pain flashed white behind her eyes. When she spat, the sink caught a red string and thinned it to nothing.

Her phone buzzed a third time and went still.

Leela rinsed once more, folded the damp packet shut, and shoved it back into the tote without checking the phone. She stayed there facing the mirror while the torn place in her cheek kept filling and emptying, filling and emptying, and the fluorescent lights hummed over the sinks like a machine too small to care what it was preserving.
